engine.summary: DynaCharting today is a PURE RENDERER with zero data-transform/compute capability. The browser path (@repo/dc-wasm) wraps the C++ core compiled to WASM: a JSON CommandProcessor (control plane) builds a flat scene graph of panes/layers/drawItems/buffers/geometries/transforms, and a binary IngestProcessor (data plane) fills anonymous typed byte-buffers via a 13-byte-header record format (op append/updateRange). 10 Dawn render pipelines draw those buffers, each requiring an EXACT vertex format (8 formats total). The ONLY transform is a per-DrawItem column-major mat3 baked from tx/ty/sx/sy (pan/zoom) plus a baked clip-space->pixel scissor. There is NO named-field data model, NO scale/domain, NO encoding/channel layer, and NO compute pipeline anywhere (grep for @compute/ComputePipeline/Dispatch returns nothing). Buffers carry FINISHED geometry exactly matching a pipeline's vertex stride; any reshaping (layout, binning, FFT, tessellation, colormapping) must happen upstream, and per-pixel color comes in as a producer-rasterized texture uploaded via setTexturePixels (the texturedQuad escape hatch). This is the exact "negative space" the hypothesis targets.

## DynaCharting Engine Inventory (today's substrate)

Repo root: `/home/ndrandal/workspace/Github/DynaCharting`. The browser/WASM path is `@repo/dc-wasm` = the C++ `dc` core compiled to WASM + the Dawn renderer (`dc_gpu`) via emdawnwebgpu. Two planes: CONTROL (JSON commands → `CommandProcessor` → `Scene`) and DATA (binary records → `IngestProcessor` → anonymous byte-buffers). No third plane exists.

### 1. Pipeline / primitive catalog (10 pipelines, 8 vertex formats)

Registered in `core/src/pipelines/PipelineCatalog.cpp` (constructor). Each `PipelineSpec` (`core/include/dc/pipelines/PipelineCatalog.hpp`) = name, version, `requiredVertexFormat`, `DrawMode`, `verticesPerInstance`. The CommandProcessor (`validateDrawItem`, lines 542–605) HARD-REJECTS a drawItem whose geometry format ≠ the pipeline's required format, and enforces vertexCount % 3 (triangles) / % 2 (lines) / ≥1 (points/instanced).

| Pipeline@v | VertexFormat (enum) | stride B | DrawMode | vpi | What it draws |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `triSolid@1` | Pos2_Clip (1) | 8 | Triangles | 0 | Flat-color triangle soup (clip-space verts) |
| `line2d@1` | Pos2_Clip (1) | 8 | Lines | 0 | GL line list (1px, no AA) |
| `points@1` | Pos2_Clip (1) | 8 | Points | 0 | Point sprites (pointSize) |
| `instancedRect@1` | Rect4 (2) | 16 | InstancedTriangles | 6 | Axis-aligned rects from (x0,y0,x1,y1); cornerRadius |
| `instancedCandle@1` | Candle6 (3) | 24 | InstancedTriangles | 12 | OHLC candle: 12 verts/instance = body quad (0–5) + wick quad (6–11) from (x,open,high,low,close,halfWidth); colorUp/colorDown |
| `textSDF@1` | Glyph8 (4) | 32 | InstancedTriangles | 6 | SDF glyph quads from (x0,y0,x1,y1,u0,v0,u1,v1); needs GlyphAtlas |
| `lineAA@1` | Rect4 (2) | 16 | InstancedTriangles | 6 | Anti-aliased thick line segments as instanced quads; dash/gap |
| `triAA@1` | Pos2Alpha (5) | 12 | Triangles | 0 | Edge-fringe AA triangles (x,y,alpha) |
| `triGradient@1` | Pos2Color4 (6) | 24 | Triangles | 0 | Per-vertex RGBA gradient triangles (pos@0, color@+8); color rides the vertex, NOT a uniform |
| `texturedQuad@1` | Pos2Uv4 (7) | 16 | InstancedTriangles | 6 | Textured quad sampling an uploaded texture (heatmap/spectrogram escape hatch) |

Vertex formats defined in `core/include/dc/scene/Geometry.hpp` (`enum VertexFormat`, `strideOf`). The buffer is a raw byte blob; the geometry's `format`+`vertexCount` tell the backend how to read it. **There is no field naming** — bytes must already be in the exact packed layout the pipeline's vertex shader expects.

Backends: `core/src/gpu/Dawn<Name>Backend.cpp` (one `IRendererBackend` per pipeline) + `DawnPickBackend`. Walked by `DawnSceneRenderer` (`core/src/gpu/DawnSceneRenderer.cpp`): clear → per-pane scissor → per-item cull/blend/clip/transform → draw. In the WASM host, if no GlyphAtlas/atlas is supplied, `textSDF@1`/`texturedQuad@1` register conditionally (host supplies an in-memory TextureSource so texturedQuad works; textSDF atlas is null → skipped) — see `core/wasm/dc_engine_host.cpp` `ensureRenderer()`.

### 2. Ingest / binary record format + buffer model

Record header = **13 bytes** (`core/src/ingest/IngestProcessor.cpp`, `HEADER_SIZE`; mirrored in `apps/showcase/src/scene/commands.ts` `encodeUpload`):
```
[1B op][4B bufferId u32 LE][4B offsetBytes u32 LE][4B payloadBytes u32 LE][payload...]
```
- `op==1` APPEND: `buf.data.insert(... pos, pos+payloadLen)`, then `enforceCap` (ring eviction if over `maxBytes`). Emits a write range (full-range if eviction shifted the buffer, else just the appended tail).
- `op==2` UPDATE_RANGE: resizes to `offset+payloadLen` if needed (zero-fills the gap), `memcpy` in place, dirty range = `[min(oldSize,offset), needed)`.
- `processBatch` loops records until `pos+HEADER_SIZE > end`; truncated payloads count as `droppedBytes`.

Buffers are `std::unordered_map<Id, CpuBuffer{ data: vector<uint8_t>, maxBytes }>`. **Anonymous, untyped, no columns** — just bytes keyed by integer id. Default cap `DEFAULT_MAX_BYTES`; eviction is front-erase (ring). Control commands `bufferSetMaxBytes`/`bufferEvictFront`/`bufferKeepLast` manage caps. `syncBufferLengths` mirrors `data.size()` into the Scene `Buffer.byteLength`. On the render side, `CpuBufferStore` (`core/include/dc/render/CpuBufferStore.hpp`) holds the render-side bytes with dirty-range coalescing + `UploadStats`, driven device-agnostically by `uploadDirty(GpuDevice&, BufferHandleResolver&)`. The WASM host `syncAllBuffers()` copies every non-empty ingest buffer into the store before each render/pick.

### 3. Command / manifest schema (every verb)

`CommandProcessor::applyJson` dispatch (`core/src/commands/CommandProcessor.cpp` lines 128–176). 33 commands. Frame transaction: `beginFrame`/`commitFrame` (always allowed) snapshot active→pending and atomic-swap on commit; any failure inside a frame sets `frameFailed_` and rejects the rest (`FRAME_REJECTED`). IDs: explicit (`reserve`) or auto (`allocate`) via `ResourceRegistry`.

- **Lifecycle/frame:** `hello`, `beginFrame{frameId?}`, `commitFrame{frameId?}`
- **Scene graph create:** `createPane{id?,name?}`, `createLayer{paneId,id?,name?}`, `createDrawItem{layerId,id?,name?}`, `delete{id}` (cascades pane→layers→drawItems)
- **Data resources:** `createBuffer{byteLength,id?}`, `createGeometry{vertexBufferId,vertexCount,format?,indexBufferId?,indexCount?,id?}` (format strings: pos2_clip/rect4/candle6/glyph8/pos2_alpha/pos2_color4/pos2_uv4)
- **Bind/validate:** `bindDrawItem{drawItemId,pipeline,geometryId}` (runs `validateDrawItem`)
- **Transforms:** `createTransform{id?}`, `setTransform{id,tx?,ty?,sx?,sy?}`, `attachTransform{drawItemId,transformId(0=detach)}`
- **Buffer cache:** `bufferSetMaxBytes{bufferId,maxBytes}`, `bufferEvictFront{bufferId,bytes}`, `bufferKeepLast{bufferId,bytes}`
- **Geometry mutation:** `setGeometryVertexCount`, `setGeometryBuffer`, `setGeometryBounds{minX,minY,maxX,maxY}` (cull AABB), `setGeometryIndexBuffer`, `setGeometryIndexCount`
- **DrawItem mutation:** `setDrawItemPipeline{drawItemId,pipeline?,geometryId?}`, `setDrawItemColor{r,g,b,a}`, `setDrawItemStyle{...}` (color, colorUp/colorDown, pointSize, lineWidth, dashLength, gapLength, cornerRadius, blendMode∈normal/additive/multiply/screen, isClipSource, useClipMask), `setDrawItemVisible{visible}`, `setDrawItemTexture{textureId}`, `setDrawItemAnchor{anchor(9 enum),offsetX,offsetY}`, `setDrawItemGradient{type∈none/linear/radial,angle,color0,color1,center,radius}`
- **Pane:** `setPaneRegion{clipXMin/Max,clipYMin/Max}` (clip-space region), `setPaneClearColor{r,g,b,a,enabled?}`
- **Text/annotations:** `ensureGlyphs{text}` (ASCII codepoints → atlas), `setAnnotation{drawItemId,role,label,value}`, `removeAnnotation{drawItemId}` (a11y metadata store, not rendered geometry)

The showcase TS side (`apps/showcase/src/scene/commands.ts`) deliberately keeps `SceneCommand = {cmd:string}&Record<string,unknown>` open — the C++ core is the authoritative validator. A `SceneManifest` = `{label, commands[], textures?[], uploads?[]}`. The TS file's own comments name the texturedQuad colormap and the upstream-precompute as "the frontier wall" / "walled-tier escape hatch."

### 4. Scene graph

`Scene` (`core/src/scene/Scene.cpp`, types in `core/include/dc/scene/Types.hpp`) = six flat `unordered_map`s keyed by Id: panes, layers, drawItems, buffers, geometries, transforms. Hierarchy is by id reference only (layer.paneId, drawItem.layerId), 2 levels deep. Cascading delete (deletePane→deleteLayer→drawItems). No retained data semantics beyond render attributes.
- **DrawItem** fields (`Types.hpp` 67–117): pipeline string, geometryId, transformId(0=identity), color[4], colorUp/Down[4], pointSize, lineWidth, dash/gap, cornerRadius, BlendMode, isClipSource/useClipMask (stencil clip), textureId, anchorPoint+offsets, visible, gradient(type/angle/2 colors/center/radius).
- **Transform** = `TransformParams{tx,ty,sx,sy}` → column-major `mat3[9]` via `recomputeMat3` (axis-aligned scale+translate ONLY — no rotation/shear/projection; it's literally pan/zoom). Each backend resolves `di.transformId` to a mat3 (or identity), confirmed in `DawnTriSolidBackend.cpp` lines 18–23.
- **Pane** = `PaneRegion{clipXMin/Max,clipYMin/Max}` in clip space → pixel scissor (`DawnSceneRenderer.cpp` ~line 330), clearColor, hasClearColor. Per-pane mid-pass "clear" emulated as a scissored fill quad (WebGPU has no scissored mid-pass clear). Pane borders/separators are synthetic fill rects.
- **Clip/blend:** stencil masks (isClipSource writes stencil / useClipMask reads it); 4 blend modes mapped to device blend.
- **Picking:** GPU color-ID pick via `DawnPickBackend` → `DawnSceneRenderer::renderPick` → returns `drawItemId` (0 = miss). Surfaced through the host `pick(w,h,x,y)`.
- **Text SDF:** `GlyphAtlas` (shelf-packed SDF glyphs); `ensureGlyphs` populates; textSDF@1 samples it.
- **Textures:** uploaded CPU pixels (R8 or RGBA8) keyed by textureId, sampled by texturedQuad@1. Lazily turned into a wgpu::Texture; re-uploading the same id swaps it (animated textures).

### 5. WASM EngineHost surface

Two layers. (a) C++ `DcEngineHost` embind class (`core/wasm/dc_engine_host.cpp`, `EMSCRIPTEN_BINDINGS` lines 368–404). Exported methods: `applyControl(jsonText)→{ok,error}`, `applyDataBatch(bytes)`, `setTexturePixels(textureId,pixels,w,h,format)`, `render(w,h)→int` (ASYNCIFY; framebuffer readback), `pick(w,h,x,y)→drawItemId`, `dispose`, `framebuffer()` (typed_memory_view), `framebufferWidth/Height`, `renderMessage`, `backend`, `stats`, `paneCount/layerCount/drawItemCount/bufferCount/geometryCount`, `listResources()` (JSON), `getBufferBytes(id)`, `bufferSize(id)`. It owns `Scene + ResourceRegistry + CommandProcessor + IngestProcessor + CpuBufferStore + MemTextureSource + lazy DawnSceneRenderer`.

(b) TS `EngineHost` wrapper (`packages/dc-wasm/src/EngineHost.ts`) — engine-host-parity API: `init(canvas)`, `start()`, `shutdown()/dispose()`, `enqueueData(ArrayBuffer)` / `applyDataBatch`, `setTexturePixels(id,bytes,w,h,format)`, `applyControl(json|obj)`, `markDirty()`, convenience `createTransform/setTransform/attachTransform/createBuffer/deleteDrawItem/...`, `getBufferBytes`, `pick`/`pickAsync`, `getStats`/`getLastErrors`, `setDebugToggles`. It buffers control/data/textures until the async WASM module loads, serializes renders (single async GPU op due to ASYNCIFY), and blits the offscreen framebuffer to a 2D canvas via `putImageData`. The only data→GPU paths are `applyDataBatch` (geometry bytes) and `setTexturePixels` (raster pixels). No method accepts named data, scales, encodings, or transforms.

### 6. Compute / transform capability — NONE

Strictly render. Confirmed by absence:
- `grep -rn "@compute"`, `ComputePipeline`, `CreateComputePipeline`, `DispatchWorkgroups`, `computePass` over `core/` and `packages/dc-wasm/` → **zero hits**. The only "Storage" usage in `core/src/gpu/` is C++ `attrStorage` (vertex-attribute scratch vectors in `DawnDevice.cpp`), not GPU storage buffers.
- The only GPU-side numeric op is the per-DrawItem `mat3` (pan/zoom) applied in each vertex shader. The fragment side does colormaps/gradients/SDF but nothing data-derived.
- There IS a `core/src/data/` and `core/src/math/` directory (CandleAggregator, AggregationManager, DerivedBuffer, Indicators, Ema, NiceTicks, AutoScale, etc.) — CPU-side helpers — but these are NOT wired into the WASM EngineHost or the CommandProcessor command set. The browser engine surface (embind) exposes none of them; the only data entry point is the raw binary `applyDataBatch`. So even the CPU compute that exists in the `dc` library is dark from the manifest/engine-host perspective.

**Net:** the manifest declares structure + style + which packed buffer feeds which pipeline; raw numbers must already BE the vertex bytes (or a rasterized texture). Every data→visual transform (layout, binning, aggregation, tessellation, FFT, KDE, marching-squares, colormapping) currently lives upstream. There is no DATASET/column model, no SCALE/domain (the baked mat3 is the entire "scale"), no ENCODING/channel binding, and no in-engine TRANSFORM/compute stage. That is exactly the gap the hypothesis proposes to fill.
keyFacts: 10 render pipelines registered in core/src/pipelines/PipelineCatalog.cpp: triSolid@1, line2d@1, points@1, instancedRect@1, instancedCandle@1, textSDF@1, lineAA@1, triAA@1, triGradient@1, texturedQuad@1.
8 vertex formats with fixed strides in core/include/dc/scene/Geometry.hpp: Pos2_Clip(8B), Rect4(16B), Candle6(24B), Glyph8(32B), Pos2Alpha(12B), Pos2Color4(24B), Pos2Uv4(16B).

CommandProcessor (core/src/commands/CommandProcessor.cpp) HARD-REJECTS geometry whose format != pipeline.requiredVertexFormat (validateDrawItem, lines 570-576) — buffers must be pre-packed to the exact pipeline layout.
Binary ingest record header is 13 bytes: [1B op][4B bufferId LE][4B offsetBytes LE][4B payloadBytes LE][payload]; op 1=APPEND, op 2=UPDATE_RANGE (core/src/ingest/IngestProcessor.cpp, HEADER_SIZE=13).
Buffers are anonymous untyped byte vectors keyed by integer Id (IngestProcessor CpuBuffer = std::vector<uint8_t> + maxBytes); NO named columns, NO data types.

The ONLY data transform applied at render time is a per-DrawItem column-major mat3 baked from {tx,ty,sx,sy} (scale+translate, no rotation/shear) — recomputeMat3 in core/include/dc/scene/Types.hpp lines 39-43; baked pan/zoom only.
NO compute capability: grep for @compute / ComputePipeline / CreateComputePipeline / DispatchWorkgroups over core/ and packages/dc-wasm/ returns zero hits; the renderer is render-pass only.
Scene graph (core/src/scene/Scene.cpp) is six flat unordered_maps: panes, layers, drawItems, buffers, geometries, transforms; hierarchy is 2-level by id reference (layer.paneId, drawItem.layerId).
33 control commands total in CommandProcessor::applyJson dispatch (lines 128-176); frame transactions via beginFrame/commitFrame with snapshot+atomic-swap and frameFailed rejection.

WASM embind surface (core/wasm/dc_engine_host.cpp EMSCRIPTEN_BINDINGS) exposes only: applyControl, applyDataBatch, setTexturePixels, render, pick, dispose, framebuffer accessors, stats, count queries, listResources, getBufferBytes, bufferSize — no data-model/scale/encoding/transform entry points.

Per-pixel color for heatmaps/spectrograms enters as producer-rasterized RGBA8/R8 pixels via setTexturePixels bound to texturedQuad@1 (the texture escape hatch); commands.ts comments call this 'the frontier wall' / 'walled-tier escape hatch'.

texturedQuad@1 registers in the WASM host (in-memory TextureSource supplied), but textSDF@1's GlyphAtlas is null in the host so SDF text is skipped there (core/wasm/dc_engine_host.cpp ensureRenderer()).

CPU-side data/math helpers exist in core/src/data (CandleAggregator, AggregationManager, DerivedBuffer) and core/src/math (Indicators, Ema, AutoScale) but are NOT wired into the WASM EngineHost embind surface or the CommandProcessor command set — dark from the manifest path.
Picking is GPU color-ID based: DawnPickBackend -> DawnSceneRenderer::renderPick -> drawItemId (0 = miss), surfaced via host pick(w,h,x,y).

CpuBufferStore (core/include/dc/render/CpuBufferStore.hpp) holds render-side bytes with dirty-range coalescing + UploadStats, uploaded device-agnostically via uploadDirty(GpuDevice&, BufferHandleResolver&); host syncAllBuffers() mirrors ingest buffers each frame.
openQuestions: Index-buffer support exists in the schema (createGeometry indexBufferId/indexCount, setGeometryIndexBuffer/Count) and Geometry struct, but I did not confirm whether any Dawn backend actually issues indexed draws vs. ignoring indexCount — worth verifying before relying on indexed geometry as a primitive.

The mat3 is the entire transform stage, but I did not verify whether the per-pane scissor + clip-space region constitutes a usable 'scale range' substrate (i.e., whether a future SCALE primitive could lower onto pane region + mat3 without new GPU state).

CPU data/math modules (AggregationManager, DerivedBuffer, CandleAggregator, Indicators) are present in dc but unexposed; whether they are mature/correct enough to be promoted into in-engine TRANSFORM primitives (vs. rewritten) is unassessed.

Whether the binary record format's single u32 bufferId/offset space and front-eviction ring is adequate for a columnar DATASET model (multiple named columns per logical table) or would need a new addressing scheme.

textSDF text layout: the engine consumes pre-positioned glyph quads (Glyph8 x0,y0,x1,y1,u,v) — text shaping/positioning is entirely upstream; the cost of moving even basic label layout in-engine is not characterized here.

gpu.summary: DynaCharting is render-only today: there is ZERO compute-shader code anywhere in the core (no CreateComputePipeline, no ComputePass, no @compute WGSL, no Storage buffer usage). The Dawn/WebGPU stack it uses — both native Dawn and the browser emdawnwebgpu port — fully supports compute (it's the same wgpu::Device/Queue already live in DawnDevice), so adding a compute stage is a pure additive engineering task, not a platform-capability gap. The WASM engine host (dc_engine_host) already has every prerequisite: a live device/queue, an offscreen render loop with a clean insertion point between data-ingest and render, a CpuBufferStore, and a working ASYNCIFY async-pump (waitUntil) that compute readback would reuse. Critically, DynaCharting ALREADY runs a CPU-side data-transform layer (core/src/data: CandleAggregator, LodManager, TemporalFilter, DerivedBuffer; core/src/recipe: SMA/RSI/MACD/Bollinger indicators emitting geometry) — this is the negative-space precedent and the lowest-risk path for v1 of the manifest transform layer. GPU compute is the right tool only for the heavy, embarrassingly-parallel tail (KDE, FFT/STFT, contour, large-N binning/aggregation), and even there the established viz prior art (M4/min-max bands, per-frame OHLC binning) triggers compute on viewport-change, not every frame. Hard WebGPU limits to design around: no f64 (f32 only), 256 invocations/workgroup, 16KB workgroup storage, 128MiB storage-buffer binding, 8 storage buffers/stage, no dynamic allocation.

## 1. Does the Dawn/WebGPU stack expose compute, and can the WASM build dispatch it?

### Platform: YES, fully. Engine: render-only today (zero compute code).

**Repo evidence — no compute exists:**
- Exhaustive grep of `core/` for `ComputePass | ComputePipeline | createComputePipeline | ShaderStage::Compute | @compute | BufferUsage::Storage` returns **zero hits** (the only "Storage"/"dispatch" matches are unrelated: `attrStorage` vertex-attribute scratch and the render-pass "draw dispatch" comment in `DawnSceneRenderer.cpp`).
- `GpuDevice` seam (`core/include/dc/render/GpuDevice.hpp`) is **render-only**: methods are createBuffer/updateBuffer/createTexture/createPipeline (render pipeline)/createBindGroup/beginRenderPass/draw/drawInstanced/readPixel. There is **no** createComputePipeline, no dispatchCompute, no compute-pass concept.
- All buffers are created with `kStreamBufferUsage = Vertex | Index | CopyDst | CopySrc` (`DawnDevice.cpp:25`). **No buffer carries `BufferUsage::Storage`** — a hard blocker that must be added before any compute shader can bind a buffer as read-write storage.
- `PipelineDesc` (`GpuDevice.hpp:239`) only models vertex+fragment WGSL, vertex layouts, topology, blend, clip. No compute-shader field, no workgroup dims, no storage-binding declarations.

**Platform capability — compute is available, unused:**
- `DawnDevice` already holds a live `wgpu::Device device_` and `wgpu::Queue queue_` (`DawnDevice.hpp:246-247`), obtained natively (dawn::native::Instance) or in-browser via emdawnwebgpu's `navigator.gpu` RequestAdapter/RequestDevice (`DawnDevice.hpp:34-47`). `wgpu::Device::CreateComputePipeline()` and `wgpu::CommandEncoder::BeginComputePass()` are members of the SAME `webgpu_cpp.h` API already in use — nothing new to link, no new dependency.
- **Browser/WASM (emdawnwebgpu): compute is fully exposed.** emdawnwebgpu implements the complete `webgpu.h`/`webgpu_cpp.h` on top of the browser's WebGPU JS API, including `createComputePipeline`, compute passes, and `dispatchWorkgroups`. The `dc_engine_host` target links `--use-port=emdawnwebgpu` (`core/CMakeLists.txt:2058`) with `-sASYNCIFY=1` already enabled — the exact flag compute-buffer readback (mapAsync) needs.
- **Readback already solved.** `DawnDevice::waitUntil()` (`DawnDevice.cpp`) already implements the async map-pump for both native (`device_.Tick()` + `wgpuInstanceProcessEvents`) and browser (`emInstance_.ProcessEvents()` + `emscripten_sleep(0)` under ASYNCIFY). A compute pass that needs results back on CPU reuses this verbatim; a compute pass that feeds a render pass GPU-to-GPU needs no readback at all.

**Bottom line:** The stack can dispatch compute today. The engine simply never asked it to. Adding compute is additive plumbing on an already-capable device — not a platform port.

## 2. WebGPU compute feasibility in-browser (2025-2026) — limits and prior art

**Maturity:** As of 2026 WebGPU compute is broadly shipped — Chrome/Edge/Firefox desktop, Safari 26 (macOS/iOS/iPadOS/visionOS), Chrome Android 12+. Storage buffers (large read-write GPU memory for raw data / histograms / prefix sums) and compute passes are first-class.

**Hard limits to design the transform basis around (WebGPU default/guaranteed minimums):**

| Limit | Default minimum | Impact on transforms |
|---|---|---|
| `maxComputeInvocationsPerWorkgroup` | **256** | workgroup size ≤256 (use 64 as the portable default) |
| `maxComputeWorkgroupStorageSize` | **16384 bytes** (16KB) | shared-memory reductions/histograms must tile to ≤16KB |
| `maxComputeWorkgroupsPerDimension` | **65535** | huge dispatches must tile across dimensions |
| `maxStorageBufferBindingSize` | **128 MiB** | single raw-data column ≤128MiB per binding (chunk larger) |
| `maxStorageBuffersPerShaderStage` | **8** | ≤8 storage buffers per compute pass → constrains multi-column transforms |
| f64 / double precision | **NOT supported** | WGSL scalars are bool/i32/u32/f32/f16 only; financial/time-precision math that needs f64 must stay on CPU or use split-f32 tricks. Time axes (epoch-ms) overflow f32 mantissa (~16.7M) — a real correctness trap. |
| Dynamic allocation | **NOT supported** | output buffer sizes must be known/bounded before dispatch — variable-output transforms (filter that removes rows, layout that emits unknown counts) need a 2-pass count-then-write or a worst-case allocation. |
| Atomics | u32/i32 only (no float atomics in core) | histogram/aggregate accumulation in f32 needs atomic-CAS loops or integer reinterpretation. |

**Data-viz-on-compute prior art (confirms the pattern is proven, not speculative):**
- **M4 / min-max-band downsampling**: compute pass reads world-space points → outputs per-screen-pixel min/max y bands → render pass draws overview geometry. The canonical "compute produces an instance/vertex buffer the render pass consumes" structure. 1.2M-point series sustained ≥60Hz, often 120Hz.
- **Per-frame OHLC/candle binning, histograms, M4 summaries** in compute passes is documented industry practice; guidance is to **trigger heavy aggregation on viewport-change, not every frame** (compute when the domain/zoom changes, render every frame from the cached result).
- **Image histogram** via compute (workgroup-shared bins + atomic merge) is a standard tutorial — directly applicable to density/heatmap binning.
- Academic: WebGPU for scalable client-side aggregate visualization (TU Wien 2023) — filtering+aggregating millions of spatio-temporal points interactively via compute.

This maps cleanly onto the "walled tail" the showcase precomputed: KDE density, STFT/FFT spectrogram, marching-squares contour, treemap/sankey layout, large-N binning — all are known GPU-compute workloads (with the caveat that irregular layout like squarified treemap and sankey ribbon routing are poor GPU fits; see open questions).

## 3. Alternative: transforms in the WASM C++ core (CPU) — tradeoffs

**DynaCharting already does this.** There is a real, shipping CPU transform layer — this is the most important architectural finding:
- `core/src/data/CandleAggregator.cpp` — aggregates raw candle6 records into coarser candles (OHLC merge, min/max high/low) — exactly the bin/aggregate transform the manifest model needs.
- `core/src/data/AggregationManager.cpp`, `LodManager.cpp`, `ResolutionPolicy.cpp`, `TemporalFilter.cpp`, `DerivedBuffer.cpp`, `VirtualRange.cpp` — LOD/decimation, time filtering, derived columns, windowed virtual ranges.
- `core/src/recipe/` — SMA, RSI, MACD, Bollinger, Stochastic, Volume recipes compute indicators **on CPU** and emit finished geometry buffers (e.g. `MacdRecipe.cpp` computes `histogram = MACD - signal` and generates rect4 bars).

These are CPU C++ transforms (raw numbers → derived numbers → geometry) running in the same WASM module — a working proof that the "data→visual" layer can live in the C++ core, single-threaded, today.

**CPU (WASM C++) vs GPU compute tradeoffs for a streaming engine:**

| Dimension | CPU (WASM core) | GPU compute (WGSL) |
|---|---|---|
| Effort to add | **Low** — extends existing `dc/data` + recipe patterns; no seam change | **High** — new GpuDevice methods, storage buffers, compute PSOs, WGSL authoring |
| Correctness/precision | f64 available; deterministic | f32-only; time-axis precision traps; nondeterministic float-atomic order |
| Latency for small/incremental N | **Lower** (no dispatch/sync overhead; append-only tail updates are trivial) | dispatch + barrier overhead dominates for small N |
| Throughput for large N (>100K–1M) embarrassingly-parallel | Worse (single WASM thread; no SIMD by default) | **Much better** (M4/histogram/KDE/FFT) |
| Readback cost | none (already CPU-side) | mapAsync stall unless kept GPU-resident into the render pass |
| Streaming fit (per-tick append) | **Excellent** — recompute only the touched tail | Re-dispatch whole pass unless carefully incrementalized |
| Algorithm fit (irregular: treemap/sankey layout) | **Good** (serial recursion is natural) | Poor (irregular, data-dependent recursion) |
| WASM threads/SIMD | Possible but engine binding is single-threaded today (`CMakeLists.txt:1853`) | N/A |

**Recommendation:** CPU-in-WASM is the correct **v1** of the manifest transform layer (filter/formula/bin/aggregate/stack/sort/window + irregular layout). GPU compute is a **v2 accelerator** for the parallel-heavy tail (KDE, FFT/STFT, contour marching, million-point M4/binning, density heatmaps) — best when output stays GPU-resident and feeds the render pass directly, triggered on viewport/domain change rather than per-frame.

## 4. What must be added architecturally to support a manifest-declared compute stage

**Insertion point is clean and already obvious in `dc_engine_host.cpp`:** the render loop is `applyDataBatch(bytes)` → mirror touched buffers into `CpuBufferStore` (`store_.setCpuData`) → `renderer_->render(scene_, store_, w, h)`. A **transform/compute pass slots between the store-sync and the render walk** — a new stage that reads named columns and writes derived columns/geometry the existing backends already know how to draw.

**For a CPU transform stage (v1) — small additive work:**
1. A `DatasetStore` / named-typed-column model fronting `CpuBufferStore` (today buffers are anonymous-byte by id; the manifest needs named f32/i32/timestamp columns).
2. A `TransformGraph` driven by the manifest (filter/formula/bin/aggregate/stack/sort/window nodes), reusing the `CandleAggregator`/`DerivedBuffer`/recipe code as the implementation substrate.
3. Scale primitives (column domain→visual range, auto-domained) to replace the single baked pan/zoom mat3.
4. Encoding bindings (markChannel ← scale(column)) that emit into the existing pipeline vertex buffers.
   - **Risk: Low.** No GpuDevice/seam change, no new dependency, mirrors shipping patterns.

**For a GPU compute stage (v2) — moderate, well-bounded work:**
1. **Seam extension** (`GpuDevice.hpp`): add `BufferUsage::Storage` to buffer creation (or a `createStorageBuffer`), `ComputePipelineDesc` (WGSL compute module + workgroup dims + storage-binding layout), `createComputePipeline`, `beginComputePass`/`dispatch(x,y,z)`/`endComputePass`, and compute-capable bind groups. ~1 new descriptor struct + ~5 virtuals + DawnDevice impls (native + emscripten share the same `webgpu_cpp` calls — one implementation, both targets).
2. **Compute backends** mirroring the per-pipeline render backends (`DawnTriSolidBackend` etc.): one per transform kind (bin, histogram, prefixScan, M4, KDE, FFT). Each authors WGSL and a dispatch shape.
3. **Buffer lifecycle**: storage buffers that are compute-written then bound as vertex buffers by the render pass (add `Vertex|Storage` usage) — keeps data GPU-resident, avoiding readback. For CPU-needed results, reuse `waitUntil()` async map.
4. **Two-pass count-then-write** harness for variable-output transforms (no dynamic alloc).
   - **Risk: Moderate.** Main hazards: f32 time-axis precision (must split-encode or keep time on CPU), WGSL authoring/debugging surface, the 8-storage-buffer / 16KB-workgroup tiling constraints for multi-column or large reductions, and the manifest→WGSL generation path (this is where the "custom-WGSL-from-JSON escape hatch" question lives — a JSON-declared compute transform either selects from a fixed library of pre-authored WGSL kernels (safe) or generates/accepts WGSL text (powerful but a validation/security surface)).

**Effort estimate:** CPU v1 transform layer ≈ a small project (several ≤5h tickets: column model, transform nodes, scale/encoding, manifest parse). GPU compute seam + first 2-3 kernels ≈ a comparable but riskier project, justified only once a specific transform is proven too slow on CPU at target data sizes.
keyFacts: ZERO compute code in DynaCharting core: grep of core/ for ComputePass|ComputePipeline|createComputePipeline|ShaderStage::Compute|@compute|BufferUsage::Storage returns no hits — the engine is render-only.
The GpuDevice seam (core/include/dc/render/GpuDevice.hpp) has no createComputePipeline/dispatch/compute-pass — only render-pipeline + draw + readPixel methods.
All buffers use kStreamBufferUsage = Vertex|Index|CopyDst|CopySrc (DawnDevice.cpp:25); NO buffer has BufferUsage::Storage — a hard prerequisite for any compute shader to bind a buffer.

DawnDevice already holds a live wgpu::Device + wgpu::Queue (DawnDevice.hpp:246) on which wgpu::Device::CreateComputePipeline() / BeginComputePass() exist in the same already-linked webgpu_cpp.h — no new dependency needed to add compute.

The browser build (dc_engine_host) links --use-port=emdawnwebgpu with -sASYNCIFY=1 (core/CMakeLists.txt:2058); emdawnwebgpu fully exposes WebGPU compute (CreateComputePipeline, compute passes, dispatchWorkgroups).

DawnDevice::waitUntil() already implements the async map-readback pump for native (Tick+ProcessEvents) and browser (ProcessEvents + emscripten_sleep(0) under ASYNCIFY) — compute readback reuses it unchanged.

DynaCharting ALREADY runs a CPU transform layer in C++: core/src/data/CandleAggregator.cpp aggregates raw candles (OHLC bin/aggregate); LodManager/TemporalFilter/DerivedBuffer; core/src/recipe computes SMA/RSI/MACD/Bollinger/Stochastic and emits geometry (MacdRecipe computes histogram=MACD-signal).
Engine render loop in dc_engine_host.cpp is applyDataBatch -> store_.setCpuData -> renderer_->render(); a transform/compute stage slots cleanly between store-sync and the render walk.
WebGPU has NO f64 (WGSL scalars: bool/i32/u32/f32/f16) — epoch-ms time axes overflow f32 mantissa (~16.7M), a real precision trap that favors keeping time math on CPU.

WebGPU default compute limits to design around: 256 invocations/workgroup, 16384-byte (16KB) workgroup storage, 65535 workgroups/dimension, 128MiB storage-buffer binding, 8 storage buffers per shader stage; no dynamic allocation; core atomics are u32/i32 only (no float atomics).

Proven viz-on-compute prior art: M4/min-max-band downsampling (compute->render-buffer, 1.2M points at 60-120Hz), per-frame OHLC binning, image-histogram via workgroup-shared bins; established practice triggers heavy aggregation on viewport-change, not every frame.

WASM engine binding is single-threaded today (CMakeLists.txt:1853) — CPU transforms get no WASM-thread parallelism by default, making GPU compute the throughput win for large-N embarrassingly-parallel transforms.
openQuestions: Manifest->WGSL path: does a JSON-declared GPU transform select from a fixed pre-authored kernel library (safe, bounded) or generate/accept arbitrary WGSL text (the 'escape hatch')? The latter is a validation/security/portability surface (WGSL translator fuzzing is an active threat area).

Irregular layout transforms (squarified treemap, sankey ribbon routing, marching-squares contour stitching) are poor GPU-compute fits due to data-dependent recursion/serial dependencies — do these stay CPU-only even in a GPU-compute future, leaving the basis hybrid rather than uniformly GPU?

Time-axis precision: with no f64, how does a universal engine represent epoch-time domains on GPU — split-f32 (hi/lo) encoding, per-frame CPU rebasing, or keep all time math CPU-side? This affects whether scale primitives can be GPU-resident at all.

Variable-output transforms (filter that drops rows, layout that emits an unknown count) need two-pass count-then-write or worst-case allocation under WebGPU's no-dynamic-allocation rule — does the manifest model bound output sizes statically, and what is the cost?

Streaming/incremental updates: per-tick tail-append is cheap on CPU but a GPU compute pass typically re-dispatches the whole transform — is incremental GPU compute (recompute only touched region) feasible for the target transforms, or does GPU only pay off for viewport-triggered full recompute?

What concrete data sizes / FPS targets justify GPU compute over the existing CPU path? The CPU layer already handles candle aggregation and indicators; the crossover point (N where single-thread WASM CPU misses frame budget) is unmeasured in this repo.

universe.summary: I enumerated ~90 chart/graph types across 9 families to serve as the adversarial universality test corpus, capturing for each its required raw-data shape and the core data→visual computation. The vast majority (roughly 70%) reduce to a small, composable basis: DATASET columns → SCALE (domain→range) → data-TRANSFORM (filter/formula/bin/aggregate/stack/sort/window) → MARK channel encoding, all of which are embarrassingly parallel and map cleanly to existing dc_gpu pipelines + per-frame WASM/WebGPU-compute. The hard tail is small but real and clusters around four computational primitives the proposed basis does NOT yet name: (1) iterative/optimization layout (force graphs, t-SNE/UMAP — fixed-point relaxation across frames), (2) constrained recursive space-filling layout (squarified treemap, sunburst, sankey/dendrogram node placement — sequential, data-dependent recursion), (3) spatial/topological extraction (marching-squares contours, Delaunay/Voronoi, hexbin, convex hull, isosurface — produce variable-length topology, not fixed-count marks), and (4) geographic map projection + polygon ingest (choropleth/cartogram need pre-baked boundary geometry + a projection function). These resist a pure stateless declarative manifest because they are stateful, iterative, recursive, or produce data-dependent variable-cardinality output — strongly implying universality requires either named heavyweight layout/topology transform primitives or a custom-WGSL-compute escape hatch. The current showcase confirms this: every walled view (treemap, sankey, contour, spectrogram) ships 180–280 lines of upstream geometry generation precisely because these computations have no in-engine primitive today.
universe.markdown:
## Chart/Graph Universe — Adversarial Test Corpus for Universality

This is the full target space "any chart or graph" must cover. For each I give the **raw data shape** (what numbers/relationships the feed actually carries — NOT geometry) and the **core data→visual computation** the engine must perform live. Difficulty is rated against the *working-hypothesis basis* (DATASET columns + SCALE + ENCODING + transforms {filter, formula, bin, aggregate, stack, sort, window} + MARK {point, rect, line, area, rule, text, arc}). 

**Difficulty legend:**
- **E (Easy)** — direct encode: scale(column)→channel, no transform or one stateless transform. Maps to an existing pipeline 1:1.
- **M (Medium)** — needs one of the named statistical transforms (bin/aggregate/stack/window/sort) — parallel, stateless-per-output, single-pass or two-pass.
- **H (Hard)** — needs a transform NOT in the proposed basis but still feed-forward/parallelizable (KDE, quantile, 2D binning, FFT, contour cells). Cardinality of output may differ from input.
- **X (eXtreme / walled)** — iterative, recursive, stateful, or variable-topology output. Does not fit a stateless declarative manifest without a named heavyweight layout/topology primitive or a custom-compute escape hatch.

### Family 1 — Statistical distributions

| Chart | Raw data shape | Core data→visual computation | Diff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bar / column | category col + value col (or raw rows to aggregate) | group-by category → aggregate(sum/mean) → rect: x=band(cat), y=linear(value) | M |
| Grouped/stacked bar | category × series × value | group-by 2 keys → aggregate → **stack** (cumulative offset) or dodge (sub-band) | M |
| Histogram | one numeric col (raw samples) | **bin** numeric domain into N buckets → count per bin → rect | M |
| Box plot | numeric col per group | per group compute min/q1/median/q3/max + outlier test (**quantile** aggregate) → rule+rect+point | H |
| Violin | numeric col per group | per group **KDE** (kernel density estimate over a grid) → mirrored area | H |
| Density (1D KDE) | numeric samples | KDE: convolve samples with kernel over output grid (N×K) → line/area | H |
| ECDF | numeric samples | **sort** + cumulative rank/N → step line | M |
| Q–Q plot | one or two numeric samples | sort both (or sample vs theoretical quantile fn) → pair by rank → point | M |
| Heatmap (matrix) | (row-key, col-key, value) triples | pivot to grid → color=scale(value) → instancedRect | E/M |
| Hexbin | x,y point cloud | **hexagonal 2D bin** (axial coord assignment) → count → hex mark | H |
| Contour (density) | x,y point cloud | 2D KDE → grid → **marching squares** isoline extraction → variable polylines | X |
| 2D density / binned heatmap | x,y point cloud | 2D rectangular bin → count grid → color rect | M/H |

### Family 2 — Time series

| Chart | Raw data shape | Core data→visual computation | Diff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Line | (t, value) | scale t→x, value→y → line2d/lineAA | E |
| Area / band | (t, value) or (t, lo, hi) | two y-scales → area fill between baseline (or lo/hi) | E |
| Multi-line | (t, series, value) | partition by series → line per group, color=scale(series) | E |
| Candlestick | (t, o,h,l,c) | map 4 prices → instancedCandle (body rect + wick rule); color=sign(c−o) | E |
| OHLC bars | (t, o,h,l,c) | open/close ticks + high-low rule → instancedRect/rule | E |
| Horizon | (t, value) | band-slice value range, fold negatives, overplot layered area (**window/offset**) | M/H |
| Streamgraph | (t, series, value) | stack with wiggle/silhouette baseline optimization (**stack variant minimizing variance**) → stacked area | H |
| Ridgeline | (t/x, group, value) | per-group KDE or line + vertical offset per group | H |
| Calendar heatmap | (date, value) | date→(week, weekday) grid mapping → rect color | M |
| Candlestick + indicators (MA/Bollinger/RSI) | OHLCV | **rolling window** aggregates (SMA, stdev bands), recursive EMA | M (EMA stateful) |

### Family 3 — Part-to-whole

| Chart | Raw data shape | Core data→visual computation | Diff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pie / donut | category + value | normalize to angular fraction → cumulative angle (**stack in polar**) → arc | M |
| Treemap | tree (parent→child + leaf value) | **squarified recursive layout** — minimize rect aspect ratio, sequential data-dependent subdivision | X |
| Sunburst | hierarchy + value | recursive radial partition: per-ring angular extent from subtree sums → arc | X |
| Icicle | hierarchy + value | recursive rectangular partition by depth → rect | X |
| Mosaic / Marimekko | 2 categorical + count | nested proportional rect: col width=marginal, row height=conditional → rect | H |
| Waffle | category + value | value→cell count → grid placement (row/col modulo) → rect | M |

### Family 4 — Hierarchy & network

| Chart | Raw data shape | Core data→visual computation | Diff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Node-link (force) | nodes + edges (id, source, target) | **force-directed iterative simulation** (repulsion O(n²)/Barnes-Hut + spring + integrate over frames) → point+line | X |
| Node-link (layered/Sugiyama) | DAG | layer assignment + crossing minimization + coordinate assignment (NP-hard heuristics) | X |
| Tree / dendrogram | hierarchy (+ merge heights for cluster) | **tidy-tree (Reingold-Tilford) recursive layout** → x by leaf order, y by depth/height → line | X |
| Arc diagram | nodes (1D order) + edges | place nodes on a line, edges as semicircle arcs (radius from endpoints) → arc | M |
| Chord | square matrix (flows i→j) | normalize to circular arcs + quadratic-Bézier ribbons between arc segments | H/X |
| Sankey / alluvial | nodes + weighted flows | **layered node layout** (assign layer, order to reduce crossings, height∝throughput) + **ribbon tessellation** | X |
| Adjacency matrix | edge list / matrix | optional **reordering** (seriation/clustering for block structure) + rect color | H (M if order fixed) |

### Family 5 — Multidimensional

| Chart | Raw data shape | Core data→visual computation | Diff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scatter | (x, y[, size, color]) | independent scales per channel → point | E |
| Bubble | (x, y, size) | size→**area** scale (sqrt) → point | E |
| Scatterplot matrix (SPLOM) | N numeric cols | all pairwise (i,j) cells = small scatters + **repeat/facet** layout | M |
| Parallel coordinates | N numeric cols | per-axis independent normalize → polyline across parallel axes (one line per row) | M |
| Radar / spider | entity × N metrics | per-axis normalize → radial coordinate (angle=axis, r=value) → closed polygon | M |
| Andrews curves | N numeric cols | per row evaluate Fourier series f(t)=x1/√2+x2 sin t+x3 cos t… over t-grid → line | H |
| t-SNE / UMAP projection | high-dim vectors | **iterative gradient-descent embedding** (neighbor graph + attraction/repulsion over many epochs) | X |
| PCA / MDS projection | high-dim vectors / distance matrix | linear: covariance + eigendecomposition (or SVD) → project to 2D → point | H |

### Family 6 — Geographic

| Chart | Raw data shape | Core data→visual computation | Diff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Choropleth | (region-id, value) + **boundary polygons** | **map projection** of polygon vertices + region tessellation + color=scale(value) | X |
| Symbol / bubble map | (lat, lon, value) | projection(lat,lon)→(x,y) → sized point | H (needs projection fn) |
| Flow / connection map | (origin, dest, weight) lat/lon | project endpoints + great-circle/Bézier arc → line | H |
| Hexbin map | (lat, lon) points | project → hex 2D bin → count → hex | H |
| Cartogram | (region, value) + geometry | **iterative area-equalizing distortion** (Gastner-Newman diffusion) of polygons | X |
| Contour map (isarithmic) | scattered (lat,lon,z) | interpolate to grid + marching squares + projection | X |

### Family 7 — Scientific / 3D

| Chart | Raw data shape | Core data→visual computation | Diff |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3D surface | z=f(x,y) grid | grid→mesh triangulation + 3D camera projection + lighting/normals | X (3D pipeline) |
| Heatmap surface (2.5D) | grid of z | color + optional height displacement | M/H |
| Volume render | 3D scalar field | **ray-marching / transfer function** through voxels | X |
| Isosurface | 3D scalar field | **marching cubes** → triangle mesh at threshold | X |
| Vector / quiver field | grid of (x,y,u,v) | per-cell arrow glyph: angle=atan2(v,u), len=|vec| → rule/arc | M |
| Streamlines | vector field | **iterative integration** (RK4) of seed particles through field → polyline | X |

### Family 8 — Domain-specific (financial & signal)

| Chart | Raw data shape | Core data→visual computation | Diff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Depth ladder / order book | (price, bid_size, ask_size) | sort by price + cumulative depth (**running sum**) → horizontal bars | M |
| Volume profile | (price, volume) trades | **bin by price** → sum volume → horizontal histogram; POC = argmax | M |
| Market/TPO profile | (price, time, trade) | per-price-bucket time-bracket letter accumulation → rect/text grid | H |
| Footprint | (price-bin, time-bin, bid_vol, ask_vol) | 2D bin (price×time) → per-cell delta + imbalance flag → rect+text | M/H |
| Renko | (t, price) tick stream | **path-dependent brick generation** (emit brick only when price moves ≥ box size; stateful sequential) | H/X |
| Point & figure | tick stream | similar stateful column/reversal logic | H/X |
| Spectrogram | audio/time samples | **STFT**: window + **FFT** per frame → magnitude → time×freq color grid | H |
| Waterfall (spectral) | repeated spectra over time | stack FFT frames into pseudo-3D ridge | H |
| Waterfall (financial) | sequential +/- deltas | **running cumulative sum** → floating rect per step | M |
| Gantt | (task, start, end[, dependency]) | map intervals → rect by row; optional dependency arrows + critical-path | M (H w/ scheduling) |
| Funnel | ordered stages + counts | stage value → centered width → trapezoid (rect/area) | E/M |
| ECG / continuous waveform | high-rate (t, mV) stream | windowed scrolling line + optional R-peak detection (window/threshold) | E/M |

---

## What the basis covers vs. what it misses

**Covered well (E/M, ~55–60 types):** Everything that is *encode + a stateless or single-window aggregate*. The proposed transforms {filter, formula, bin, aggregate, stack, sort, window} plus marks {point, rect, line, area, rule, arc, text} and per-channel scales cover all of Family 1 (except violin/contour), most of Family 2, Family 5 scatter/parcoord/radar, and most financial profile charts. These are embarrassingly parallel — ideal for WebGPU compute or per-frame WASM, and they map onto the 10 existing dc_gpu pipelines directly. This is the strong core of the universality claim.

**The hard tail clusters around FOUR computational primitives NOT named in the working hypothesis:**

1. **Iterative / fixed-point relaxation** — force-directed graphs, t-SNE/UMAP, cartograms, streamlines, EMA-style recursions. Output of frame N depends on frame N−1; they converge over many iterations. A *stateless* declarative manifest cannot express "run this until equilibrium." This is GPU-friendly (compute shaders, see GraphWaGu / cosmos.gl) but requires the engine to own persistent simulation state and a per-frame integrate step — i.e., a **named iterative-layout primitive** or a custom-compute loop. **Hardest, most universal gap.**

2. **Constrained recursive space-filling layout** — squarified treemap, sunburst, icicle, tidy-tree/dendrogram, sankey node placement, Sugiyama layering. These are *sequential, data-dependent recursions* with global constraints (minimize aspect ratio, minimize edge crossings). Hard to parallelize, hard to express declaratively because the control flow branches on intermediate results. In this repo, treemap (`records.gen.mjs`, 258 lines) and sankey (232 lines) ship exactly this as upstream geometry — direct evidence of the gap.

3. **Spatial / topological extraction (variable-cardinality output)** — marching squares (contours), marching cubes (isosurfaces), Delaunay/Voronoi, hexbin, convex hull. Input is N points; output is M polylines/triangles where M is data-dependent and unknown at author time. The mark-channel model assumes one-mark-per-row or fixed-grid; variable topology breaks that. Needs a **topology-producing transform primitive** that emits a new dataset of geometry. Repo evidence: contour (188 lines) and spectrogram (279 lines) precompute this upstream.

4. **Geographic projection + boundary geometry** — choropleths/cartograms/flow maps need (a) pre-existing boundary polygons (raw data is just region-id→value; the *shapes* are reference geometry, not in the data feed) and (b) a non-affine **projection function** (Mercator, Albers, orthographic) replacing the baked affine mat3. This is a clean named primitive (projection scale + a geometry-join transform) but it is fundamentally more than the current affine transform allows.

**Secondary frictions:**
- **Polar/radial coordinate systems** (pie, radar, chord, sunburst, radial-seasonality) need a coordinate transform the current affine-only stage lacks — but this is a *closed-form* coordinate primitive, not iterative. Medium lift.
- **3D** (surface, volume, isosurface) needs a camera/projection + depth pipeline entirely absent today.
- **FFT/STFT** (spectrogram, waterfall) is feed-forward and GPU-parallel but is a specialized signal transform — a named `fft` transform primitive, not expressible from {bin, aggregate}.
- **Path-dependent emission** (renko, point&figure) — output rows are generated by a stateful scan over the stream (emit-on-threshold). This is a `scan`/stateful-window transform, between window and full iteration.

## Implication for the universality question

A pure stateless declarative manifest over {filter, formula, bin, aggregate, stack, sort, window} + marks reaches roughly two-thirds of the corpus cleanly. Full universality additionally requires naming **at least these heavyweight primitives**: `iterate/simulate` (force, embedding), `recursiveLayout` (treemap/hierarchy/sankey), `contour/triangulate` (topology extraction), `project` (geographic + 3D camera), `fft`, `coordinate` (polar/radial), and `scan` (path-dependent emission). Each is real and bounded — but several (especially iterative layout and recursive constrained layout) are stateful or branch on intermediate data, which is exactly what a declarative JSON manifest is bad at. This is the strongest argument that universality needs a **custom-WGSL-compute escape hatch from JSON**: rather than enumerate every exotic layout as a built-in, expose user-authored compute stages so the long tail (and future chart types) is reachable without engine changes. The built-in primitive set should cover the E/M/H mass; the escape hatch covers the X tail.
keyFacts: ~90 chart types enumerated across 9 families; roughly two-thirds (E/M difficulty) reduce to the proposed basis: DATASET columns + SCALE + ENCODING + {filter, formula, bin, aggregate, stack, sort, window} + marks {point, rect, line, area, rule, arc, text}.

The hard tail clusters into exactly 4 computational primitives NOT in the working-hypothesis basis: (1) iterative/fixed-point layout, (2) constrained recursive space-filling layout, (3) spatial/topological extraction with variable-cardinality output, (4) geographic projection + boundary-polygon join.

Iterative-layout charts (force graph, t-SNE/UMAP, cartogram, streamlines) are stateful across frames — frame N depends on frame N-1 — which a stateless declarative manifest cannot express; they are GPU-friendly (GraphWaGu, cosmos.gl prove WebGPU/WebGL compute-shader force layout at 100k+ nodes) but need engine-owned simulation state.

Recursive constrained layouts (squarified treemap, sunburst, icicle, tidy-tree, sankey node placement, Sugiyama layering) branch control flow on intermediate results; squarified treemap (Bruls/Huizing/van Wijk 2000) minimizes rectangle aspect ratio via sequential data-dependent subdivision.

Topology-extraction charts (marching squares contour, marching cubes isosurface, Delaunay/Voronoi, hexbin, convex hull) produce data-dependent variable-count geometry, breaking the one-mark-per-row / fixed-grid encoding model.

Geographic charts need TWO things absent from the data feed: pre-baked boundary polygon geometry (raw data is only region-id->value) and a non-affine projection function (Mercator/Albers/orthographic) to replace DynaCharting's baked affine pan/zoom mat3.

Repo evidence of the gap: every 'walled' showcase view ships hundreds of lines of upstream geometry generation - treemap records.gen.mjs = 258 lines, sankey = 232, spectrogram = 279, contour = 188 (paths under apps/showcase/views/).

Vega-Lite's documented expressiveness limits align with the same tail: it struggles with custom marks, composite graphics, and intricate layouts; its gamut is a strict subset of full Vega (source: IDL/UW Vega-Lite InfoVis 2017 paper).
Polar/radial charts (pie, radar, chord, sunburst) need a closed-form coordinate-transform primitive the current affine-only stage lacks - a medium lift, distinct from the iterative tail.
FFT/STFT (spectrogram, waterfall) and path-dependent emission (renko, point&figure via stateful scan) are additional named transforms not derivable from {bin, aggregate, window}.
openQuestions: Where is the cut line between built-in primitives and a custom-WGSL escape hatch? Naming ~7 heavyweight primitives (iterate, recursiveLayout, contour/triangulate, project, fft, coordinate, scan) may cover the X tail without an escape hatch — but each new exotic chart risks needing another. Quantify how many X-types each named primitive actually unlocks.

Can iterative layouts (force, t-SNE) be made acceptable in a STREAMING engine where data changes every frame? Convergence assumes stable input; with a live feed the simulation may never settle. Does the engine run N iterations/frame and accept non-converged intermediate states?

Variable-cardinality topology output (contours, Voronoi, treemap rects) means the GPU buffer size is unknown until compute runs — does the architecture support compute stages that allocate/emit a dynamically-sized downstream dataset, or must marks be pre-sized?

For geographic charts, is shipping boundary polygons as 'reference geometry' a violation of the no-precomputed-geometry thesis, or an acceptable separate static asset class distinct from the raw data stream?

Does the proposed model need a coordinate-system primitive (cartesian/polar/geographic/3D-camera) as a first-class concept replacing the single baked affine mat3, and is that one primitive or several?

How much of the H tier (KDE, quantile, 2D-bin, FFT, Andrews/Fourier) is genuinely expressible as composed primitives vs. needing dedicated named transforms — i.e., is 'formula over a grid + aggregate' enough to synthesize KDE/FFT declaratively, or are these irreducible primitives?

priorArt.summary: Across Vega, Vega-Lite, ggplot2, D3, Observable Plot, ECharts, Plotly and scientific tools (matplotlib/VTK), the declarative grammar-of-graphics vocabulary has converged on a remarkably stable and finite basis: ~7-10 mark/geom primitives (point, line, area, rect/bar, rule, arc, text, plus geo/image), ~12 scale types (linear, log, pow/sqrt, symlog, time/utc; ordinal, band, point; quantile, quantize, threshold, bin-ordinal/sequential), and a union of ~30 data transforms (filter, formula/calculate, bin, aggregate, stack, sort, window, fold, pivot, lookup/join, sample, impute, density/KDE, regression/loess, quantile, plus harder layout transforms: stack, pie, force, treemap/pack/partition/tree, voronoi, contour, geo-projection, hexbin, wordcloud, label). This basis covers roughly 80-90% of the 22 DynaCharting showcase views as composable manifests. Critically, EVERY system reaches an expressiveness ceiling and provides an escape hatch: Vega has custom transforms + a restricted JS expression language + signals/event-streams; Vega-Lite escapes by compiling-to-Vega; ggplot2 by custom Stat/Geom ggproto objects; D3 by being raw JS; ECharts by custom-series renderItem; Plotly/matplotlib/VTK by imperative APIs. The hard tail that consistently falls OUTSIDE declarative grammars is: true 3D/volume/isosurface/streamline fields (VTK territory, no 2D grammar covers it), novel/custom marks and bespoke layout algorithms, frame-coupled animation/physics, and arbitrary spatial tessellation. This strongly implies DynaCharting's target model is sound but REQUIRES a custom-compute escape hatch (custom-WGSL-from-JSON) to be truly universal — the grammar covers the composed middle, the escape hatch covers the walled tail, exactly as every prior-art system demonstrates.
priorArt.markdown:
## Grammar-of-Graphics Prior Art: Proven Primitive Basis and Expressiveness Ceilings

This is an extraction of the PROVEN, battle-tested vocabulary from declarative-viz systems, plus a rigorous accounting of where each grammar STOPS and how it escapes. The relevance to DynaCharting: the working hypothesis (DATASET + SCALE + ENCODING + TRANSFORM + MARK primitives, AI-authored manifest, data→pixels in-engine) is essentially a re-implementation of the Vega/Vega-Lite/D3 stack on a WebGPU substrate. Prior art tells us (a) the minimal basis is finite and convergent, (b) it is NOT fully universal, and (c) universality empirically requires an escape hatch.

### 1. Consolidated MARK / GEOM table (the visual primitives)

Marks are the geometric atoms. The union is small and convergent. DynaCharting's existing ~10 pipelines already cover most of it.

| Mark primitive | Vega | Vega-Lite | ggplot2 (geom_) | D3 (d3-shape) | Observable Plot | ECharts | Existing dc_gpu pipeline | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| point / symbol | symbol | point/circle/square | point, jitter, count | symbol | Dot | scatter | points | scatter, bubble (size channel) |
| rect / bar | rect | bar | bar, col, tile, raster, rect, bin_2d | (manual) | Bar/barX/barY, Rect, Cell | bar, heatmap | instancedRect | bars, histograms, heatmap cells, candle bodies |
| line | line | line | line, path, step | line, lineRadial | Line | line | line2d, lineAA | time series |
| area / ribbon | area | area | area, ribbon, density | area, areaRadial | Area | line(areaStyle) | triSolid/triGradient | streamgraph, band, CI |
| rule / segment | rule | rule, errorbar, errorband | segment, hline, vline, abline, errorbar, linerange, pointrange, spoke, rug | (manual) | Rule, Tick, Link, Arrow | markLine | line2d | axes, ticks, OHLC whiskers, candle wicks |
| arc / wedge | arc | arc | (via coord_polar) | arc, pie | (arc via geo/special) | pie, gauge, sunburst | — (gap) | pie, donut, radial |
| text / label | text | text | text, label | (manual) | Text | (label) | textSDF | labels, annotations |
| trail | trail | (size on line) | — | — | — | — | — | variable-width line |
| image / raster | image | image | raster | (manual) | Image, Raster | — | texturedQuad | bitmap/field background |
| geo / shape / path | shape, path | geoshape | sf, map, polygon | geoPath, path | Geo | map | (gap) | choropleth, vector polygons |
| boxplot | (composite) | boxplot | boxplot, violin | (manual) | Box | boxplot | (composite) | always decomposes to rect+rule |
| candlestick / OHLC | (rule+rect) | (not native) | (manual) | (manual) | (manual) | candlestick | instancedCandle | DynaCharting has a dedicated pipeline; most grammars compose it |

**Convergence finding:** The irreducible mark basis is roughly **{point, line, area, rect, rule, arc, text}** plus **{image/raster, geo/path}** for fields and maps. ~7-9 primitives. Everything else (boxplot, candlestick, errorbar, violin, pie) is a COMPOSITE of these. DynaCharting is missing a clean `arc` and a generic `geo/path` mark; everything else maps to existing pipelines.

### 2. Consolidated SCALE table (data domain → visual range)

Scales are the single most convergent part of the grammar — every system has essentially the same set. This is the layer that REPLACES DynaCharting's baked affine pan/zoom mat3.

| Scale type | Vega | Vega-Lite | D3 | ggplot2 | Maps to | 
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| linear | linear | quantitative | scaleLinear | scale_*_continuous | continuous→continuous |
| log | log | log | scaleLog | scale_*_log10 | |
| pow / sqrt | pow, sqrt | pow, sqrt | scalePow, scaleSqrt | scale_*_sqrt | |
| symlog | symlog | symlog | scaleSymlog | — | signed log (zero-crossing) |
| time / utc | time, utc | temporal | scaleTime, scaleUtc | scale_*_date/datetime | |
| sequential / diverging | sequential, (diverging via scheme) | (color schemes) | scaleSequential, scaleDiverging | scale_*_gradient/gradient2 | continuous→color |
| ordinal | ordinal | nominal | scaleOrdinal | scale_*_discrete/manual | discrete→discrete |
| band | band | (x/y nominal) | scaleBand | (discrete position) | discrete→position w/ width |
| point | point | (x/y nominal) | scalePoint | (discrete position) | discrete→position no width |
| quantile | quantile | quantile | scaleQuantile | scale_*_binned | continuous→discrete by quantile |
| quantize | quantize | quantize | scaleQuantize | scale_*_steps | continuous→discrete uniform |
| threshold | threshold | threshold | scaleThreshold | scale_*_manual breaks | continuous→discrete by breaks |
| bin-ordinal | bin-ordinal | bin | (bin scale) | — | binned ordinal |

**Convergence finding:** ~12-13 scale types, identical across systems. Add **color interpolation spaces** (rgb, hsl, lab, hcl, cubehelix — all linear-on-color) and **named color schemes** (viridis, category10, RdBu, etc.). For DynaCharting this is the critical missing primitive: an auto-domained `scale(column) → visual range` that recomputes every frame on streaming data (the "nice domain" + d3-style domain inference). This is cheap to evaluate in WASM or as a GPU uniform-per-mark; it directly displaces the single mat3.

### 3. Consolidated TRANSFORM table (the data→data layer — the actual GAP in DynaCharting)

This is the heart of the question. Transforms split cleanly into **tabular** (cheap, row-wise/groupwise, trivially WASM or GPU-compute) and **layout/spatial/spectral** (the hard tail that DynaCharting currently precomputes upstream).

#### 3a. Tabular transforms (the "easy" basis — covers the long head of charts)

| Transform | Vega | Vega-Lite | D3 | Plot | ggplot2 stat | GPU-compute feasible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| filter | filter | filter | (.filter) | filter / select | — | trivial (predicate) |
| formula / calculate | formula | calculate | (manual) | map | — | trivial (per-row expr) |
| bin / histogram | bin, dotbin | bin | bin | bin | stat_bin/bin2d | trivial (atomic add) |
| aggregate | aggregate | aggregate | rollup, group | group | stat_summary | reduction (groupby) |
| joinaggregate | joinaggregate | joinaggregate | (manual) | (group+map) | — | groupby + broadcast |
| window | window | window | (manual) | window | — | prefix-scan / sliding |
| stack | stack | stack | stack | stack | stat_count(pos=stack) | segmented scan |
| sort | collect(sort) | sort (encoding) | sort | sort | — | sort (GPU radix) |
| fold / pivot | fold, pivot | fold, pivot | (manual) | — | (tidyr) | reshape (index math) |
| flatten | flatten | flatten | (manual) | — | — | reshape |
| lookup / join | lookup | lookup | (Map join) | (manual) | — | hash/index join |
| impute | impute | impute | — | (manual) | — | gap-fill |
| sample | sample | sample | (manual) | — | — | reservoir |
| extent | extent | extent | extent | (auto) | — | min/max reduction |
| sequence | sequence | sequence | range | — | — | generator |
| timeunit | timeunit | timeUnit | (d3-time) | interval | — | per-row date floor |
| identifier / nest | identifier, nest | — | — | — | — | id assignment |
| normalize | (stack normalize) | (stack normalize) | — | normalize | — | groupby + divide |
| dodge | — | — | — | dodge | position_dodge | 1D packing |
| shift | — | — | — | shift | — | offset |

#### 3b. Statistical transforms (still declarative, moderately hard)

| Transform | Vega | Vega-Lite | D3 | ggplot2 | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| density / KDE (1D) | density, kde | density | (manual) | stat_density | DynaCharting precomputes this today (ridgeline) |
| KDE 2D / heatmap density | kde2d, heatmap | (density) | contourDensity | stat_density_2d | density-heatmap view — precomputed |
| regression (linear/poly) | regression | regression | (manual) | stat_smooth(lm) | |
| loess | loess | loess | (manual) | stat_smooth(loess) | |
| quantile / ECDF | quantile | quantile | quantile | stat_ecdf, stat_qq | |

#### 3c. Layout / spatial / spectral transforms (the HARD TAIL — what DynaCharting precomputes upstream)

| Transform | Vega has? | D3 module | DynaCharting showcase view it would replace | Difficulty in-engine |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| stack (areas) | stack | d3-shape stack | streamgraph | easy (scan) |
| pie / arc layout | pie | d3-shape pie | (radial) | easy |
| treemap (squarified) | treemap | d3-hierarchy treemap | **treemap** (precomputed today) | medium — iterative, recursive layout |
| pack (circle) | pack | d3-hierarchy pack | — | medium-hard (iterative relaxation) |
| partition (icicle/sunburst) | partition | d3-hierarchy partition | — | medium |
| tree / cluster | tree | d3-hierarchy tree | — | medium (Reingold-Tilford) |
| stratify / nest | stratify, nest | d3-hierarchy stratify | — | easy (build tree) |
| force-directed | force | d3-force | (network) | HARD — iterative N-body physics, frame-coupled |
| voronoi / delaunay | voronoi | d3-delaunay | — | hard (Fortune's / incremental) |
| sankey ribbon layout | (no native — plugin) | d3-sankey (plugin) | **sankey** (precomputed today) | hard — iterative iteration + ribbon tessellation |
| contour (marching squares) | contour, isocontour | d3-contour | **contour** (precomputed today) | medium — GPU-friendly (per-cell) |
| hexbin | (no native) | d3-hexbin (plugin) | footprint-ish | easy (lattice snap) |
| geo projection | geopath, geopoint, geoshape, graticule | d3-geo | weather-radar/maps | medium (per-vertex transform) |
| wordcloud | wordcloud | (plugin) | — | hard (greedy collision packing) |
| label placement | label | (plugin) | (all annotated views) | hard (collision avoidance) |
| linkpath | linkpath | d3-shape link | sankey/tree edges | easy |
| crossfilter / resolvefilter | crossfilter, resolvefilter | (crossfilter.js) | (interaction) | medium (bitset indexing) |
| STFT / FFT / spectrogram | **none** | **none** | **spectrogram, audio, ecg** (precomputed) | HARD — no grammar has this; pure signal-processing |
| marching cubes / isosurface | **none** | **none** (2D only) | — (3D) | OUTSIDE all 2D grammars |

**Critical finding:** Vega's transform list (~40 transforms incl. geo+hierarchy+layout) is the most complete declarative basis in existence and it ALREADY INCLUDES treemap, pack, partition, tree, contour, isocontour, kde2d, force, voronoi, pie, stack — i.e. most of what the DynaCharting showcase precomputed upstream. This is strong positive evidence that the "data→pixels in-engine" target is achievable for ~18 of the 22 views. The transforms NO grammar covers: **STFT/FFT/spectral** (spectrogram, audio, ECG-frequency) and **anything truly 3D**.

### 4. Streaming / reactive model (the real-time crux for DynaCharting)

DynaCharting's differentiator is per-frame streaming. Prior art for this is **Reactive Vega** (Satyanarayan et al.), the most relevant precedent:

- **Vega signals + event streams**: a dataflow graph of reactive `signal` values driven by `event-streams` (mouse, timer ticks, signal updates). Updates propagate through the transform DAG incrementally. This is the closest existing model to "recompute data→pixels every frame on a live feed."
- **Vega-Lite** has no streaming primitive of its own — it compiles down to Vega and inherits the dataflow. There is also research work "Animated Vega-Lite" (MIT) adding animation as a grammar dimension (time-encoding channel), but it is not in mainstream Vega-Lite.
- **ggplot2, Observable Plot, Plotly, matplotlib**: essentially STATIC/batch — re-render the whole spec on data change; no incremental dataflow, no streaming primitive. ECharts has `setOption` merge + `appendData` for streaming but recomputes layout.
- **Implication for DynaCharting**: The reactive-dataflow-DAG model (transform nodes that incrementally update on append/updateRange) is proven by Reactive Vega. DynaCharting's existing `op append / updateRange` byte-buffer model maps naturally onto a Vega-style dataflow if each transform node is made incremental. The GPU-compute angle (running the transform DAG as compute passes) is novel vs. all prior art (which runs transforms on CPU/JS) — this is genuinely new ground, not copied.

### 5. WHERE GRAMMARS BREAK / HOW THEY ESCAPE

Every declarative system has a hard ceiling and an escape hatch. This is the single most important finding for the architecture question — it predicts DynaCharting WILL need a `custom-WGSL-compute-from-JSON` escape hatch.

| System | Expressiveness ceiling (what it canNOT do declaratively) | Escape hatch |
|---|---|---|
| **Vega** | No 3D. No custom geometric marks beyond the 12. Expression language is a **restricted JS subset**: NO assignment, NO `++`, NO `new`, NO `for/while/switch` (no loops, no arbitrary control flow). No FFT/spectral. No arbitrary new layout algorithm. | (a) **Custom transforms** via the Extensibility API (write JS, register a Transform); (b) expression language for row-wise formulas/signals; (c) full imperative JS around the runtime. |
| **Vega-Lite** | Limited to its fixed marks + encoding channels; "less expressivity and fine-grained control than D3" (its own docs/Wikipedia). No custom marks, no composite custom layouts, no 3D. | **Compile down to Vega** and edit the Vega spec; or drop to D3. |
| **ggplot2** | Fixed coord systems (cartesian, polar/radial, map, sf, fixed, flip, trans) — no arbitrary projections without coord_*. No 3D (needs separate packages: rgl, plotly). No interactivity/streaming. | Write a **custom Stat/Geom/Coord as a ggproto object** (full R code), or custom `stat_manual`. |
| **D3** | Not a grammar at all — it's the escape hatch itself. Ceiling is developer effort, not expressiveness. No 3D (needs three.js/regl). | It IS raw JavaScript; unlimited. |
| **Observable Plot** | "No chart types, only marks" — but fixed mark+transform set; no custom marks without dropping to D3; no 3D. | Drop to **D3 / custom render functions**. |
| **ECharts** | Built-in series cover ~standard charts across coord systems (cartesian2d, polar, geo, radar, parallel, single, calendar). Anything novel isn't expressible via options. | **Custom series `renderItem(params, api)`** — imperative function returning graphic element defs, using `api.coord()`/`api.size()`/`api.style()`. Full escape to per-datum drawing. |
| **Plotly** | ~40 fixed trace types incl. 3D (scatter3d, surface, mesh3d, isosurface, volume, cone, streamtube). Beyond trace types → nothing. | Imperative JS API; or shapes/annotations; ultimately custom WebGL. |
| **matplotlib** | Static, imperative-by-default; mplot3d is limited (painter's algorithm, no true depth/volume). | It's imperative Python; or hand off to VTK/Mayavi. |
| **VTK** | This IS where 2D grammars stop — true 3D scientific viz: volume rendering, isosurface (marching cubes), streamlines/streamtubes in vector fields, cutting planes, glyphs. **No declarative grammar covers this domain at all.** | VTK is an imperative pipeline (sources→filters→mappers→actors); ParaView adds a GUI but it's still a procedural pipeline, not a grammar. |

#### The consolidated "walled tail" — things consistently OUTSIDE every declarative 2D grammar:

1. **True 3D / volumetric fields**: volume rendering, isosurface/marching-cubes, 3D streamlines, cutting planes. Owned by VTK/Plotly-3D, never by a grammar. (DynaCharting is a 2D engine, so this is arguably out of scope — but it's the clearest hard wall.)
2. **Signal-processing / spectral transforms**: FFT, STFT, spectrograms, wavelets, filtering. NO grammar has these as transforms. DynaCharting's spectrogram/audio/ECG views precompute them upstream — and would have to either add them as opaque transform primitives or use the escape hatch.
3. **Novel marks / bespoke geometry**: anything not in {point,line,area,rect,rule,arc,text,image,geo}. Every system escapes to custom-code (Vega custom transform, ECharts renderItem, ggproto Geom, D3).
4. **Novel layout algorithms**: a new graph layout, a new space-filling algorithm, a domain-specific packing. Grammars ship a FIXED menu (treemap/pack/partition/tree/force/voronoi); anything else = plugin or custom code (d3-sankey, d3-hexbin, wordcloud are all plugins, not core).
5. **Frame-coupled physics/animation/simulation**: force-directed is the canonical example — it's iterative N-body integration, inherently stateful across frames. Vega has `force` as a special transform precisely because it doesn't fit the pure-functional transform model. Animation is bolted on (Reactive Vega signals; research-grade Animated Vega-Lite).
6. **Arbitrary control flow / loops in expressions**: Vega explicitly forbids loops and assignment in its expression language for safety/determinism. Any algorithm needing real iteration must be a compiled transform (or custom code), not an inline expression.

### 6. Coverage estimate (% of charts the declarative basis covers)

- **Tabular + statistical transforms + standard scales + standard marks**: cover the classic statistical-graphics canon — bar/line/area/scatter/histogram/heatmap/boxplot/error-bands/regression/small-multiples. Industry consensus and the Vega-Lite paper position this at the vast majority (~80-90%) of everyday analytical charts.
- **+ layout transforms (treemap/pack/partition/tree/pie/stack/contour/geo/hexbin/voronoi)**: pushes coverage to the structured-data tail — hierarchies, flows, maps, density fields. Vega's transform list reaches ~18 of DynaCharting's 22 showcase views.
- **Residual ~10-15% that needs the escape hatch**: spectral (spectrogram/audio/ECG-frequency), any 3D, novel marks/layouts, and frame-coupled simulation. This is the empirically-irreducible tail that EVERY grammar offloads to custom code.

### 7. Direct implications for the DynaCharting architecture question

1. **The minimal composable basis is real and convergent**: ~9 marks, ~12 scales, ~30 transforms. It is well-proven and DynaCharting's existing pipelines already cover most marks. This validates the DATASET+SCALE+ENCODING+TRANSFORM+MARK decomposition — it is exactly the Vega model.
2. **It is NOT fully universal.** Every single prior-art system hits a ceiling and ships an escape hatch. The evidence is unanimous. Therefore DynaCharting's universality claim REQUIRES the escape hatch (custom-WGSL-compute-from-JSON is the GPU-native analog of Vega's custom transforms / ECharts' renderItem / ggproto Geoms).
3. **The hard tail is predictable**: 3D fields (out of scope for a 2D engine), spectral DSP, novel layouts/marks, and frame-coupled simulation (force). These are exactly the showcase views that were precomputed upstream — confirming the "negative space" observation.
4. **The streaming/GPU-compute dataflow is the genuinely novel contribution** — Reactive Vega proves the reactive-DAG model on CPU/JS; no prior system runs the transform DAG as WebGPU compute passes per frame. DynaCharting's append/updateRange buffer model is a natural substrate for an incremental dataflow.
5. **Layout transforms are the make-or-break engineering risk**: treemap/sankey/contour/force are iterative and not embarrassingly-parallel; implementing them as in-engine compute (vs. the easy tabular transforms) is where the project's effort concentrates. Grammars treat several of these as plugins, not core — a signal that they are genuinely hard and were added late.
keyFacts: Vega ships ~40 transforms total: basic (aggregate, bin, collect, countpattern, cross, density, dotbin, extent, filter, flatten, fold, formula, identifier, kde, impute, joinaggregate, loess, lookup, pivot, project, quantile, regression, sample, sequence, timeunit, window), geo (contour, geojson, geopath, geopoint, geoshape, graticule, heatmap, isocontour, kde2d), layout (force, label, linkpath, pie, stack, voronoi, wordcloud), hierarchy (nest, stratify, treelinks, pack, partition, tree, treemap), crossfilter (crossfilter, resolvefilter). Source: https://vega.github.io/vega/docs/transforms/

Vega/Vega-Lite scale types are convergent across systems: quantitative (linear, log, pow, sqrt, symlog, time, utc, sequential-deprecated); discrete (ordinal, band, point); discretizing (quantile, quantize, threshold, bin-ordinal). ~12-13 types. D3 matches exactly (scaleLinear/Log/Pow/Sqrt/Symlog/Time/Utc, scaleSequential/Diverging, scaleOrdinal/Band/Point, scaleQuantize/Quantile/Threshold). Source: https://vega.github.io/vega/docs/scales/, https://d3js.org/api

Vega has exactly 12 mark types: arc, area, image, group, line, path, rect, rule, shape, symbol, text, trail. The irreducible non-composite basis is ~{point, line, area, rect, rule, arc, text, image, geo/path} (~9). Boxplot, candlestick, errorbar, violin, pie all decompose to these. Source: https://vega.github.io/vega/docs/marks/

Vega-Lite encoding channels: position (x, y, x2, y2, xError, yError, xError2, yError2), offset (xOffset, yOffset), polar (theta, radius, theta2, radius2), geo (longitude, latitude, longitude2, latitude2), mark-property (color, fill, stroke, opacity, fillOpacity, strokeOpacity, strokeWidth, strokeDash, size, angle, shape), text/tooltip (text, tooltip), href, description, detail, key, order, facet (facet, row, column). Source: https://vega.github.io/vega-lite/docs/encoding.html

Vega-Lite has NO native streaming primitive of its own; it compiles to Vega and inherits Vega's reactive dataflow (signals + event-streams). Vega-Lite's own docs/Wikipedia state it has 'less expressivity and fine-grained control than D3' and is 'limited to available marks and visual encoding channels.' Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vega_and_Vega-Lite_visualisation_grammars

Vega's expression language is a RESTRICTED subset of JavaScript: no assignment operators (=, +=), no pre/postfix updates (++), no `new`, and no control-flow statements (for, while, switch). Any algorithm needing real iteration must be a compiled custom transform, not an inline expression. Source: https://vega.github.io/vega/docs/expressions/
